one_line_takeaway: Treating MCP tool descriptors as trusted metadata is a mistake: subtle, schema-valid wording changes can drive LLM agents to unsafe tool use up to 36% of the time, and only a layered protocol-level defense (integrity signing + LLM semantic vetting + runtime guardrails) meaningfully mitigates it, roughly halving unsafe invocations to 15% at a real usability cost.
key_finding: Descriptor manipulation substantially degrades safety even with valid schemas and permissions. Ablation (Table XXIV): no defense yields 36% unsafe invocations and 41% block rate; the full-stack mitigation cuts unsafe invocations to 15% and raises block rate to 74%, at a usability cost (false positives 7%->22%, latency 4.79s->6.53s, task success 93.5%->78.4% per Table XXII). Semantic vetting is the strongest single layer (block 0.64, unsafe 0.22). Shadowing is most detectable (0.75-0.97) while Tool Poisoning is hardest (0.42-0.60). LLaMA-3.5 is fastest (0.65s) but weakest at filtering; DeepSeek-V3 slowest/most variable (5.66s); GPT-5.3 most stable. Chain-of-Thought/Reflexion reduce malicious selection to 7-8% vs 13% for Scarecrow. Attacks transfer across models at 0.61-0.77 of source effectiveness.
core_contribution: The paper formalizes "descriptor-level" semantic attacks against Model Context Protocol (MCP) tool-augmented LLMs — a surface distinct from classic prompt injection because the adversary manipulates only the natural-language tool DESCRIPTORS (metadata) injected into the model's reasoning context, while tool schemas stay syntactically valid and permission-compliant. It defines three descriptor-driven attack classes (Tool Poisoning: biasing a tool's own descriptor; Shadowing: one malicious descriptor contaminating the shared context and skewing interpretation of other benign tools; Rug Pull: post-approval descriptor mutation). Its own proposed defense is a layered, protocol-level (not model-retraining) mitigation stack combining three mechanisms it labels "(ours)": descriptor integrity verification via cryptographic manifest signing (targets Rug Pull), pre-context semantic vetting by an auxiliary LLM (targets Tool Poisoning), and lightweight runtime/heuristic guardrails. NOTE on attribution: systems such as AgentDojo, ToolBench, McpSafetyScanner (Radosevich et al.), MCPSecBench, MCP-Guard, and MCP-Tox are cited Related Work, NOT contributions of this paper.
threat_model: Adversary can only manipulate natural-language tool descriptors during the MCP lifecycle — at registration and, in a relaxed setting, after approval (Rug Pull). All tools remain syntactically valid and permission-compliant; the attacker cannot modify model parameters, tool implementations, or system infrastructure, and the base LLM is assumed aligned and uncompromised (Table IV). The attack vector is purely adversarial semantics embedded in metadata aggregated into the shared MCP context, biasing tool ranking/selection without code execution or schema violation — framed as a semantic supply-chain vulnerability. Deployment context modeled is an enterprise AI copilot connected to internal services (document retrieval, corporate messaging, scheduling, secure file access), evaluated under both a controlled/validated configuration and a permissive/minimal-validation stress configuration.
limitations: By the authors' own statement: evaluation uses controlled synthetic descriptors in a testbed rather than deployed enterprise MCP systems, so empirical validation in production remains future work; no operational enterprise logs were available. Only single-step tool selection is modeled (no multi-step tool dependencies). The semantic vetting verifier shares the same general architecture class as the primary model and is itself exposed to similar manipulation, and it materially increases false positives (over-blocking benign calls), degrading task success. Independent (my) observations: the three models are given non-standard version labels (GPT-5.3, DeepSeek-V3, LLaMA-3.5) with no citation or API details, limiting reproducibility; the paper reports slightly inconsistent false-positive figures across tables (e.g., combined FP as 21.8% in Table XXI vs 21.6% in text; vetting FP 28.2% vs 18.8%); attack scenarios and descriptors are hand-constructed rather than mined from real incidents.

datasets_used: No public/standard benchmark; a controlled synthetic enterprise MCP testbed of 6 in-house tools (InternalKnowledgeSearch, DocumentRetriever, CorporateMailAssistant, CalendarWriter, ShellExec, SecureFileReader) with benign/adversarial descriptor pairs
A 420-descriptor real-world corpus for realism analysis: Anthropic MCP examples (120), LangChain tools (120), AutoGen tools (90), OpenAI tool schemas (90); mean 29.5 tokens, 24.5% risky semantic cues
Ecosystem exposure inspection of Anthropic MCP, LangChain Agents, Microsoft AutoGen, and OpenAI tool-calling (exposure scores 0.86, 0.68, 0.79, 0.56)
quantitative_results: Ablation (Table XXIV): No-Defense block 0.41 / unsafe 0.36 / FP 0.07 / 4.79s vs Full-Stack block 0.74 / unsafe 0.15 / FP 0.22 / 6.53s
Semantic Vetting alone: block 0.64, unsafe 0.22 (largest single-layer gain); Runtime Guardrails 0.53; Signed Manifest 0.49; Permission Gating 0.46; Schema Validation 0.43
Security-usability trade-off (Table XXII): Baseline MCP 41.2% block / 93.5% task success / 6.5% FP vs Full Stack 72.2% block / 78.4% task success / 21.6% FP

Per-attack block rates (Table VI): Shadowing 0.85/0.97/0.75, Rug Pull 0.73/0.64/0.59, Tool Poisoning 0.60/0.49/0.42 for GPT-5.3/DeepSeek-V3/LLaMA-3.5; ~30% of unsafe invocations bypass baseline defenses
Adaptive stress (Table IX) lowers Tool Poisoning block rates: GPT-5.3 0.60->0.45, DeepSeek-V3 0.49->0.37, LLaMA-3.5 0.42->0.30
Latency (Table XV): LLaMA-3.5 mean 0.65s, GPT-5.3 1.95s, DeepSeek-V3 5.66s (up to 45.22s max; 16.97s mean under Shadowing); two-way ANOVA scenario effect F=99.41, p<0.001, eta^2=0.149 (large)

Prompting strategy malicious tool selection under combined mitigation (Table XXV): Chain-of-Thought 7%, Reflexion 8%, Self-Critique/Instructional 9%, Few-shot/Verifier 10%, Zero-shot 11%, Scarecrow 13%
Cross-model transferability (Table XXVI): 0.61-0.77 of source attack effectiveness preserved; LLaMA-3.5-optimized descriptors transfer most strongly (0.74 to GPT-5.3, 0.77 to DeepSeek-V3)
Descriptor perturbation strength (Table XI): Low/Medium/High malicious selection 0.19/0.27/0.34 with block rate 0.69/0.72/0.79
baselines_compared: Pre-existing MCP safeguards it compares against: schema validation, permission gating, allowlist registration, user consent dialogs
Prior-work systems in the Section V / Table XXVIII comparison: McpSafetyScanner (Radosevich et al., static manifest audit), Hasan et al. (MCP server analysis), Ferrag et al. (threat taxonomy)
NOTE: semantic/LLM vetting, signed-manifest integrity verification, runtime/heuristic guardrails, and the full stack are the paper's OWN proposed mechanisms/ablation components, not baselines
